items.name: 10-year Treasury yield
items.note: eased to around 4.57% as safe-haven demand picked up alongside the broader tech selloff, even with oil spiking on renewed Middle East escalation

items.name: Crude oil (WTI)
items.note: jumped roughly 3% to above $85 after US Central Command confirmed a third service member killed and unidentified remains found near a prior strike site, deepening the Iran standoff

items.name: Gold (per oz)
items.note: holding just above $4,000, roughly flat as oil-driven inflation risk and safe-haven flows pull it in opposite directions

items.name: US Dollar Index
items.note: little changed; markets now pricing roughly a coin-flip chance of a September Fed move

items.name: Nvidia
items.note: extended its slide as China's Moonshot AI unveiled the Kimi K3 model, reviving 'DeepSeek moment' fears about US AI capex

items.name: Microsoft
items.note: pulled back with the broader AI-infrastructure complex ahead of its July 29 earnings

items.name: Alphabet
items.note: still digesting the Gemini 3.5 Pro delay reports; reports earnings Wednesday July 22

items.name: Oracle
items.note: stabilized and edged higher after last week's slide to one-notch-above-junk credit status; still down sharply from its all-time high

items.name: Taiwan Semiconductor
items.note: extending last week's decline even after its record Q2 profit; the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is down over 12% this week, its worst in 15 months

items.name: ASML Holding
items.note: fell with equipment names despite a recent guidance raise, as the Kimi K3 shock reignites questions about the pace of AI chip demand

items.name: Tesla (Optimus)
items.note: pulled back ahead of Wednesday July 22 earnings; recent reporting notes Optimus progress has lagged Tesla's own timeline

items.name: Intuitive Surgical
items.note: held up better than the broader tape, still benefiting from strong da Vinci 5 platform adoption and recurring instrument revenue

items.name: Teradyne
items.note: roughly stable after a volatile stretch tied to insider-selling headlines and overvaluation debate; reports earnings July 28

items.name: Salesforce
items.note: eased despite a fresh Guggenheim upgrade to Buy, which argued AI-disruption fears had pushed valuations too low

items.name: ServiceNow
items.note: still working through the IBM-driven enterprise-budget scare, though it also picked up a Guggenheim Buy upgrade; reports earnings July 22

items.name: Vishay Precision Group
items.note: gave back some of its recent Q1-beat-driven surge (strong AI-infrastructure and semiconductor-equipment demand) amid the broader chip pullback

items.name: Cadence Design Systems
items.note: sold off sharply alongside Synopsys after reports that a Chinese AI model autonomously designed a functional chip in 48 hours, raising fresh questions about design-tool moats

items.name: Synopsys
items.note: fell over 8% on the same Kimi K3-linked chip-design news; among the hardest-hit names in today's session

items.name: Bitcoin
items.note: ticked higher and held up better than equities, relatively insulated from today's chip-sector shock

items.name: CrowdStrike
items.note: roughly flat and still well above its tracked stop, though its valuation premium (27x forward sales vs. peers) is drawing more analyst scrutiny

items.name: Zscaler
items.note: outperformed CrowdStrike again today; trades at a far cheaper forward-sales multiple, keeping it above its tracked entry

items.name: ServiceNow
items.note: still below its pre-selloff level despite the Guggenheim upgrade

outlook.summary: Not financial advice — this is a read of the public data and news above, meant to help you decide, not a recommendation. Always size positions and confirm with your own research.

strongest.reason: Guggenheim upgraded both Salesforce and ServiceNow to Buy, arguing the AI-disruption selloff sparked by IBM's budget-reallocation warning had pushed valuations too far — a sign some of the sector's worst fears may already be priced in even though shares haven't fully recovered yet.

Intuitive Surgical continues to hold up better than the broader tape on strong da Vinci 5 adoption and recurring instrument revenue, a steadier contrast to Tesla's Optimus program, where reporting shows real-world deployment still running behind its own timeline.

weakest.reason: China's Moonshot AI unveiled the Kimi K3 model — a 2.8-trillion-parameter open-weight model reportedly competitive with top US models at a fraction of the cost — triggering a broad 'DeepSeek moment' selloff. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is down over 12% this week, and design-tool names Synopsys and Cadence fell 8-9% on reports the same model autonomously designed a working chip in 48 hours.

The group remains under pressure from a mix of overlapping worries: lingering fallout from Oracle's credit downgrade, Alphabet's delayed Gemini 3.5 Pro model, and the IBM-driven fear that AI hardware spending is cannibalizing traditional software and cloud budgets.

outlook.nextStep: The Kimi K3 shock to chips and design-tool stocks is the dominant story right now and is better watched than acted on until it's clearer whether this is a repeat DeepSeek-style overreaction or a genuine shift in the AI compute narrative. The enterprise-software rebound thesis (Guggenheim's upgrades) and this week's earnings from Alphabet, Tesla, and ServiceNow (Wed-Thu) look more worth a closer look for confirmation of the next move.

signals.title: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 triggers a second 'DeepSeek moment' for chip stocks
signals.body: Chinese startup Moonshot AI (backed by Alibaba) unveiled Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter open-weight model that reportedly matches or beats several leading US models on benchmarks while charging a fraction of the price. The news triggered a sharp selloff across the AI chip complex: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is down over 12% this week — its worst week in more than 15 months — with Taiwan's market down 6% and Japan's down 4%. Nvidia briefly lost its spot as the world's most valuable company to Apple.
signals.watch: Whether the selloff extends into this week's earnings from Alphabet, Tesla, and Intel, or stabilizes the way markets did after the original DeepSeek shock in 2025.
signals.focus: Lower conviction right now — this is a fast-moving, sentiment-driven story rather than a confirmed structural shift, worth watching closely before drawing conclusions.

signals.title: Design-tool stocks Synopsys and Cadence hit hardest by the Kimi K3 news
signals.body: Synopsys fell about 8% and Cadence Design Systems fell over 9% after reports that Moonshot's Kimi K3 model autonomously designed a functional semiconductor chip in 48 hours using open-source tools, raising questions about whether AI could erode the value of traditional chip-design software moats.
signals.watch: Whether this proves to be a durable threat to EDA software demand or an overreaction to an early-stage capability demo.
signals.focus: Lower conviction right now — a single demo doesn't establish a trend, but the market reaction shows how sensitive these names are to AI-capability headlines.

signals.title: Middle East escalation pushes oil up 3% as a third US service member is confirmed killed
signals.body: US Central Command confirmed a third American service member was killed in the ongoing conflict, and forces reported finding unidentified remains near the site of an earlier Iranian strike. WTI crude jumped roughly 3% to trade above $85 a barrel on the news, while the 10-year Treasury yield eased to around 4.57% as some money rotated into safe havens alongside the tech-sector selloff.
signals.watch: Any further escalation or de-escalation signals, and whether sustained oil gains start feeding back into inflation expectations ahead of the Fed's next meeting.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — geopolitical risk and its knock-on effects on rates and inflation are moving quickly right now.

signals.title: Guggenheim upgrades Salesforce and ServiceNow to Buy after IBM-driven selloff
signals.body: After IBM's earnings warning suggested enterprise customers are diverting budget from software toward AI hardware — a fear that hit ServiceNow, Workday, Salesforce, and Appian — Guggenheim analyst John DiFucci upgraded both Salesforce and ServiceNow to Buy, arguing the AI-disruption fear had pushed valuations too low relative to fundamentals.
signals.watch: Whether other analysts follow with similar upgrades, and how ServiceNow's July 22 earnings report addresses the budget-reallocation narrative directly.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — a specific, named analyst call pushing back on a sector-wide fear is a distinct signal from general sentiment.

signals.title: Optimus timeline slips while Intuitive Surgical's platform keeps compounding
signals.body: Reporting continues to highlight that Tesla's Optimus humanoid robot program is running behind its own internal targets, with zero units reported doing useful factory work as of the most recent update, even as the company converts more manufacturing capacity toward the program. Meanwhile, Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci 5 rollout has continued to drive strong recurring revenue and a dominant share of soft-tissue robotic surgery.
signals.watch: Whether Tesla's July 22 earnings call gives a firmer, more credible Optimus production timeline.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look for contrast — one name in this tier has a proven, monetizing platform today; the other is further out on the execution curve.

signals.title: CrowdStrike's valuation premium over Zscaler keeps widening
signals.body: CrowdStrike trades at roughly 27x forward sales versus Zscaler's 5.26x, even as both have been strong performers this year (CRWD +46% YTD versus ZS's own sharp swings). Today both were roughly flat to slightly higher, but the valuation gap between the two is becoming a more frequent topic among analysts comparing the pair.
signals.watch: Whether Zscaler's cheaper multiple starts attracting relative-value rotation if the broader software rebound continues.
signals.focus: Worth checking in on, but a large valuation gap alone isn't a signal to act on without a fresh catalyst.

signals.title: Bitcoin holds steady while equities absorb the chip-sector shock
signals.body: Bitcoin ticked up about 1% and Ethereum was roughly flat, both notably calmer than the sharp moves in chip and design-tool stocks tied to the Kimi K3 news. Options market flow reportedly still shows some traders positioning for further BTC upside this month.
signals.watch: Whether crypto continues to decouple from equity-market AI-sentiment swings or gets pulled into broader risk-off flows if the chip selloff deepens.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look as a relative-stability read, but not a strong directional signal on its own.
